posts.title: What I Eat Before Early Morning Runs
posts.excerpt: I don't need a full brunch before dawn, but I do need a plan. This is what I eat before different kinds of morning runs and what I avoid.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# What I Eat Before Early Morning Runs

Morning running sounds clean and disciplined in theory. In practice, it usually means I am standing in the kitchen half awake, trying to decide whether coffee counts as breakfast.

I've learned the answer depends on the run. What I eat before a short easy run is not what I eat before a long run or a harder workout. Once I stopped using the same routine for everything, mornings got easier.

## For Short Easy Runs

If the run is short and truly easy, I usually keep it light. Sometimes that means a banana. Sometimes a piece of toast. Sometimes just a few sips of something with carbs if I am heading out quickly.

The goal is to feel settled, not full.

## For Workouts or Longer Runs

If I know the run will be longer or the effort will be higher, I want something a little more reliable in me. That usually means:

- toast with jam or honey
- half a bagel
- oatmeal if I have enough time
- banana plus something simple on the side

I aim for food that is easy to digest and boring in a good way. Race nutrition is not the time to get creative, and neither is a 6 a.m. workout.

## Timing Matters More Than Perfect Food

If I eat too much too close to the start, I feel it. If I eat nothing before a harder session, I usually feel that too. So I try to match the amount to the time I have.

A rough version of what works for me:

- 15 to 30 minutes before: very small snack
- 45 to 60 minutes before: light breakfast
- 90 minutes or more before: fuller breakfast if needed

Nothing fancy. Just enough to keep the run from turning into a stomach experiment.

## What I Avoid

A few things have burned me before:

- too much fiber
- heavy greasy food
- trying a new gel or drink at random
- eating like I am sitting down for brunch and then leaving 20 minutes later

I have made all of those mistakes. They stay memorable.

## Coffee

Yes, I still drink coffee. I just try not to treat it like a meal. For some runners it works perfectly before a run. For others it creates its own set of logistical problems. You probably already know which group you belong to.

## Practice It Before Race Day

This is the part that matters most. If you have a race coming up, your long runs are where you figure out breakfast timing and pre-run food. That is also a good place to work through your race plan with the [Fuel Planner](/fuel).

The less decision-making you leave for race morning, the better.

## My Goal

I want to start the run feeling light, calm, and ready. Not hungry. Not stuffed. Not wondering whether I made a dumb choice in the kitchen.

That took some trial and error, but once I found a few breakfast options that worked, mornings got much simpler. And simple is good when your alarm is going off before sunrise.

posts.title: Race Morning Nerves: What Actually Helps Me
posts.excerpt: I still get nervous before races. The difference now is that I don't confuse nerves with a bad sign. Here’s what helps me settle down and race well anyway.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Race Morning Nerves: What Actually Helps Me

I still get nervous before races. That never fully disappeared. I just stopped treating the feeling like proof that something was wrong.

Before my first half marathon, the nerves were loud enough that I could barely think straight. By the time my second one came around, I still felt them, but I had more structure around the morning. That made a huge difference.

## I Keep the Morning Small

Race morning gets messy when every decision feels important. So I try to make as many choices as possible before the day starts.

The night before, I lay out:

- kit
- socks
- shoes
- bib
- gels
- watch
- breakfast plan

That way I don't wake up and start playing a dumb little game of "where did I put that one thing I need most?"

## I Stick to the Same Breakfast

This sounds boring because it is. Good. I don't want surprises. I eat something I have already used in training and I keep the timing familiar.

## I Look at the Plan, Not Every Possible Outcome

When nerves spike, my brain gets dramatic. Suddenly every scenario feels possible and none of them feel fun. What settles me down is going back to the actual plan:

- opening pace
- fuel timing
- key course points

The [Pace Calculator](/calculator) helps me lock in the pace piece. If the course has hills, I like checking it on [Elevation Finder](/elevation-finder) ahead of time so I am not surprised later.

## I Expect the Start to Feel Chaotic

Crowds, music, people darting around in warmup gear, lines for the bathroom, the weird mix of excitement and tension. I used to think I had to feel calm for the race to go well. Now I just expect the chaos and move through it.

## A Few Things That Calm Me Down

These are the small things that help me most:

- getting there early instead of cutting it close
- walking for a few minutes instead of standing frozen
- breathing slowly on purpose
- talking to someone if I'm getting too locked inside my own head

Nothing revolutionary. Just ways to stop the morning from speeding up on me.

## I Remember What the Nerves Mean

Usually the nerves mean I care. That is all. They do not predict whether the race will go well. They do not erase the training block.

Once I understood that, race morning got easier. I didn't need to beat the nerves. I just needed to keep them from driving.

## By the Time the Race Starts

Once the gun goes off, things usually get simpler. The body gets a job to do. The brain gets quieter. All the fidgety energy has somewhere to go.

So if you feel nervous on race morning, welcome to the club. Put a little structure around the morning, trust the work you've already done, and let the race begin before you decide how the day feels.

posts.title: Form Cues I Use When My Legs Start Falling Apart
posts.excerpt: I don't think about form much when I'm fresh. I do think about it late in long runs and races, when a couple simple cues can keep everything from getting sloppy.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Form Cues I Use When My Legs Start Falling Apart

I don't spend most of my runs obsessing over technique. If you try to micromanage every part of your stride all the time, running starts to feel weird fast.

But late in a race or a long run, when fatigue starts pulling everything downward, a few simple reminders help me hold it together. Not perfectly. Just enough to stay efficient.

## The Goal Is Small Corrections

When you are tired, you are not rebuilding your stride from scratch. You are making small adjustments that stop the slide. That is how I think about form now.

## Cue 1: Run Tall

This is the one I use most. When I'm fading, I tend to fold a bit through the chest and lose posture. Thinking "run tall" helps me reset without overcomplicating it.

I don't mean stiff. Just upright enough that breathing feels open and my stride stops collapsing underneath me.

## Cue 2: Relax My Hands

If my hands are tight, the rest of me usually is too. Relaxing them often calms my shoulders and arms right away.

Sometimes I picture holding a potato chip without crushing it. Corny? Sure. Still works.

## Cue 3: Quick Feet

When fatigue hits, my stride can get long and clunky. Thinking "quick feet" helps me get back to a smoother rhythm. I don't chase a specific cadence number in the moment. I just want the stride to stop reaching.

## Cue 4: Eyes Up

Looking down too much makes me feel more tired. Lifting my gaze a bit helps posture and keeps me connected to what is coming next on the course.

## Cue 5: Exhale Fully

Sometimes what feels like dead legs is really tension everywhere. A strong exhale helps me loosen up and settle back into effort.

## When I Use These Cues

Usually not when I am cruising. I use them when I notice one of these signs:

- shoulders creeping up
- breathing getting choppy
- stride feeling heavy
- pace slipping while effort spikes

That is my signal to check in instead of just fighting harder.

## I Don't Use Ten Cues at Once

One or two is plenty. Any more than that and I stop racing and start overthinking. If posture feels bad, I go to "run tall." If my stride feels sloppy, I go to "quick feet."

Simple works better when you're deep into the hard part.

## Form and Fitness Still Go Together

Good cues help, but they don't replace training. The reason those cues work better for me now is because I have spent more time running easy when I should, working on tempo sessions, and building long-run strength. The [Pace Calculator](/calculator) helped me clean up that side of training so I wasn't always showing up half-cooked.

Late-race form is partly technique and partly whether you have trained well enough to keep moving cleanly under fatigue.

Still, a couple good reminders can save a rough patch. When my legs start falling apart, I don't need a speech. I just need one clear thought and a little composure.

posts.title: Running Cadence: What It Is, Why It Matters, and How to Improve It
posts.excerpt: I spent two years running at 155 steps per minute and wondering why my legs felt heavy. Here's what I learned about cadence, the 180 myth, and how small changes in stride rate made a real difference.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Running Cadence: What It Is, Why It Matters, and How to Improve It

For the first two years I ran, I never thought about cadence. I just ran however my legs wanted to move. Turns out, they wanted to move at about 155 steps per minute, which is on the lower end of what most coaches consider efficient.

Once I started paying attention to it, my running changed. Not dramatically overnight, but gradually. Fewer impact issues, smoother stride, less grinding on hilly terrain. Here's what I've learned.

## What Is Cadence?

Cadence is your step rate—the number of steps you take per minute. Most running watches track this automatically. You'll see it listed as SPM (steps per minute) or sometimes as strides per minute (which counts only one foot, so half the SPM value).

A quick sanity check: count how many times your right foot hits the ground in 30 seconds. Multiply by 4. That's your cadence.

## The 180 SPM Myth

You've probably heard that 180 steps per minute is the magic number. This comes from Jack Daniels observing elite runners at the 1984 Olympics—virtually all of them were running at 180 or above.

Here's the problem: those were elite athletes racing, not training. And "180" became a universal prescription applied to every runner at every pace, which was never the point.

The reality:

- Elite runners average 180-200 SPM at race pace
- Recreational runners often fall between 155-170 SPM
- Lower cadences aren't automatically wrong
- Optimal cadence varies by height, leg length, pace, and experience

So no, you don't need to force yourself to 180. But if you're significantly below your natural efficient range, there's likely room to improve.

## Why Cadence Matters

Cadence affects your stride in ways that directly impact injury risk and efficiency.

### Overstriding

Low cadence often comes paired with overstriding—landing with your foot too far in front of your center of mass. When your foot strikes out in front of you, it acts like a brake. Every step, you're decelerating slightly before accelerating again.

Overstriding also increases ground reaction forces at the knee and hip. It's a common contributing factor to runner's knee, IT band issues, and shin splints.

A higher cadence naturally pulls your foot strike closer to underneath your body, reducing that braking force.

### Vertical Oscillation

Lower cadence tends to mean more bounce—more vertical movement with each step. That energy going up and down isn't moving you forward. It's wasted.

Increasing cadence slightly tends to reduce vertical oscillation and smooth out the stride.

### Muscle and Joint Load Distribution

Research consistently shows that increasing cadence by even 5-10% shifts load away from the knee and hip toward the ankle and calf. For runners with knee issues, this can be meaningful.

I had nagging knee discomfort during my first big training block. It wasn't the only cause, but improving my cadence was part of what helped.

## What's My Ideal Cadence?

There's no perfect number, but here's a practical framework:

- **Below 160 SPM**: Likely overstriding, good opportunity to improve
- **160-170 SPM**: Common range for recreational runners, some room to work with
- **170-180 SPM**: Solid range for most training paces
- **180+ SPM**: Elite and fast recreational runners at race efforts

Your cadence will also naturally change with pace. Running faster typically means higher cadence. Easy runs might sit at 165-170 while a hard tempo or interval session might push to 175-185.

Use the [Pace Calculator](/calculator) to find your training zones, then check your cadence at each effort level. The pattern is more useful than a single snapshot number.

## How to Improve Cadence

The worst way to improve cadence is to obsess over it mid-run every day. That gets exhausting and usually degrades other aspects of form.

Here's what actually works:

### 1. Start Small (5% Increase)

If you're at 160 SPM, don't try to jump to 180. Aim for 168. That's an 8-step increase that your body can adapt to without completely rebuilding your stride.

Rapid cadence changes can stress your calves and Achilles because they change how and when your ankle absorbs impact. Go gradually.

### 2. Use a Metronome

Metronome apps for runners are free and genuinely useful. Set it to your target cadence, run to the beat for a portion of your easy run. Not the whole run—just 5-10 minutes of focused work.

Over several weeks, the new rhythm becomes natural without you having to think about it.

### 3. Short Stride Drills

"Quick feet" drills—short, fast steps in place or over a flat surface—train your neuromuscular system to fire faster. Add these as part of your warmup 2-3 times per week. 20-30 seconds at a time is plenty.

### 4. Run to Music

Playlists built around a specific BPM are a low-effort way to guide cadence. A lot of runners find this more natural than a metronome because the beat sits in the background.

### 5. Hill Running

Running uphill naturally shortens stride length and increases cadence. Incorporating some hilly terrain in your training can reinforce the mechanics you're trying to build.

For course planning, our [Elevation Finder](/elevation-finder) lets you check how much gain is in your training routes.

## Cadence at Different Paces

One mistake I made early was expecting a single cadence target for all runs. That's not how it works.

| Effort | Typical Cadence Range |
|--------|----------------------|
| Easy run | 162–172 SPM |
| Tempo/threshold | 170–180 SPM |
| Interval/race pace | 178–190 SPM |

These are approximate. The point is: your easy-day cadence will naturally be lower than your race-day cadence, and that's completely fine.

## Signs You've Improved

You're not just chasing numbers. These are the real signals that cadence work is paying off:

- Smoother transitions between strides
- Less pounding on descents
- Reduced quad and knee soreness after long runs
- Easier time holding pace late in workouts
- Lighter feeling in the legs at the same effort

The improvements are subtle but they compound. After a few months of deliberate work, I noticed my form held together much better in the back half of long runs—which is when it matters most.

## Don't Chase the Number

Cadence is a useful tool, not a destination. It's one signal among many.

If your current cadence is working fine—no injury patterns, running feels efficient, times are improving—then you probably have bigger levers to pull. Prioritize pace training, easy day discipline, and fueling before spending weeks obsessing over SPM.

But if you're dealing with recurring knee or hip issues, or your stride feels clunky and overreaching, cadence is worth looking at. A few targeted adjustments can make a real difference without overhauling everything.

Start by checking your current cadence. Most running watches will have it. See where you land. If you're in the 155-165 range and running is feeling harder than it should, a small increase is worth experimenting with.

And whatever number you're at, make sure the rest of your training is dialed in too. Check your paces at [TrainPace](/calculator) and give your body the right mix of easy days, workouts, and recovery. Cadence is one piece—not the whole picture.

posts.title: Running in the Rain Without Making It Miserable
posts.excerpt: Rainy runs don’t have to be heroic and they don’t have to be awful. A few practical habits made them way easier for me to handle.

author.name: Alex
author.bio: Developer & runner. Went from 3:01 to 2:06 in the half marathon.

posts.content:
# Running in the Rain Without Making It Miserable

Living in Vancouver means I don't get to pretend rain is some rare training obstacle. If I waited for dry weather every time, I would miss a lot of running.

That doesn't mean I enjoy being soaked for no reason. I just got better at handling rainy runs so they stopped feeling like a whole event.

## Dress for Running, Not for Standing Around

This took me a while to figure out. If I dress to feel cozy before I start, I usually end up overdressed once I get moving.

For most rainy runs, I want:

- a cap to keep water out of my eyes
- light layers
- something that won't stay heavy when wet
- socks that don't turn into a swamp immediately

A giant jacket can feel great in the first minute and awful twenty minutes later.

## Protect the Things That Matter

A few small details make a big difference:

- body glide or anti-chafe where you need it
- dry clothes ready for after
- phone in a zip bag or protected pocket
- watch charged before you leave

Rain is annoying. Rain plus chafing and a dead phone is much more annoying.

## Pick a Route That Makes Sense

I keep rainy-day routes simple. Good footing, familiar turns, less traffic, less puddle roulette. If the run needs structure, I would rather repeat a boring stretch than spend the whole time dealing with slippery corners or poor lighting.

## Adjust the Goal if the Weather Demands It

If the weather is rough enough, I care less about splits and more about effort and staying upright. Same with wind. A pace that feels easy on a calm day may feel different when rain and gusts are in the mix.

That is where knowing your effort zones helps. The [Pace Calculator](/calculator) gives me a solid anchor, and then I let the conditions have their say.

## Finish the Run Properly

The run is not over when you stop your watch. If I hang around in wet clothes, the whole experience gets worse fast. Shoes off, dry socks on, warm clothes, done.

That little bit of discipline after the run is half the reason the next rainy run feels manageable.

## Rainy Runs Build Something Useful

They are rarely glamorous. Nobody is posting their best rainy-sock moment. But they do build a kind of steadiness. You learn that a run can still be good even when the conditions are annoying.

That mindset carries over to race day too. Weather does not always cooperate. Being able to stay calm and practical helps a lot more than being dramatic about it.

So no, I don't cheer when it is pouring outside. I just know how to deal with it now. That has been enough.
